claims.text: The safety classification interface has two orthogonal axes: `isConcurrencySafe(input)` (may run alongside other concurrent-safe tools) and `isReadOnly(input)` (does not mutate any state). Both are per-input methods, not static flags -- a BashTool call may be read-only (grep) or not (rm). Default for both via buildTool() is false (fail-closed).

A third safety axis `isDestructive(input)` flags irreversible operations (delete, overwrite, send). Defaults to false. This is separate from isConcurrencySafe -- a tool can be non-destructive but also not concurrency-safe (e.g. one that builds an index).

Tool classification matrix (confirmed from individual tool files): GlobTool -> isConcurrencySafe=true, isReadOnly=true. GrepTool -> isConcurrencySafe=true, isReadOnly=true. FileReadTool -> isConcurrencySafe=true, isReadOnly=true, maxResultSizeChars=Infinity. WebSearchTool -> isConcurrencySafe=true, isReadOnly=true. FileEditTool -> no isConcurrencySafe override (defaults false). FileWriteTool -> no isConcurrencySafe override (defaults false). BashTool -> isConcurrencySafe delegates to isReadOnly(input) -- dynamic based on command analysis.

BashTool's isReadOnly(input) is dynamic: it calls checkReadOnlyConstraints() with a compound-command-has-cd check. Commands like grep/find/ls qualify; git push/rm/write ops do not. This is the most sophisticated safety classification -- not a static flag but a per-invocation runtime analysis of the shell command string.

The `interruptBehavior()` method is a fourth safety dimension, independent of concurrency: it controls what happens when a new user message arrives while this tool is running. 'cancel' = stop immediately and discard result; 'block' = keep running, new message waits. Default is 'block'. This is per-tool-type, not per-invocation.

Batch-only Bash errors cascade via a siblingAbortController: only BashTool errors abort sibling tools in a concurrent batch. Read/WebFetch/etc errors do NOT abort siblings -- the comment explains 'one failure shouldn't nuke the rest' for independent reads. This is an intentional asymmetry: Bash commands often have dependency chains, reads are independent.
